result:
The first thing I notice when I wake up is the color of the sky sliding through the slats of the half-shut blinds, a bruised violet that suggests 5:47 a.m. It is not yet the syrupy gold of full morning, nor is it the star-drunk black of 3 a.m. insomnia. It is the hour when the world is still hyphenated—night-day, asleep-awake, yesterday-tomorrow—and the mind can perch on that hyphen like a sparrow on a phone wire, not obliged to fly in either direction. I lie still, feeling the sheets have grown a mild ecosystem of warmth that belongs only to me, and I think: this is the only republic I will ever truly govern.  

Outside, the neighbor’s sprinkler system hisses with the solemnity of a monk practicing whispered psalms. The sound is so measured that it becomes a metronome for my breathing. I try to synchronize, four beats of spray in, four beats out, and immediately notice how artificial it feels to choreograph the diaphragm. The body is at its most honest when it forgets it has an audience; the lungs do not care about aesthetics. They want oxygen the way a river wants the sea—without metaphor, without longing for anything except arrival. I abandon the game and let my chest do as it pleases, which feels like a small rebellion against every self-help podcast I’ve mainlined over the last decade.

A dog barks two houses away, the kind of bark that starts as a baritone announcement and fractures into a tenor question: is anybody out there, and if so, do they have treats? I do not own a dog, partly because my lease prohibits them, and partly because I fear the inevitable day when the dog will look at me with the same disappointment everyone eventually does when they realize how little I can carry. Even a cocker spaniel has expectations—walks at measurable intervals, a consistency of affection, a willingness to throw saliva-soaked tennis balls until rotator cuffs petition for early retirement. I can barely remember to water the pothos wilting on the windowsill; its leaves droop like green handkerchiefs surrendered in surrender. The plant and I are engaged in a slow staring contest of neglect. So far, the plant is winning because it still believes I might change.

I decide the day’s first act will be to rescue the pothos. I fill a jug and let the water sit for thirty seconds so the chlorine can off-gas, a tip I learned from a bearded man at the farmers’ market who spoke about root systems the way other people talk about astrology. As I pour, the soil exhales a sigh of tired perfume, earth mixed with the ghost of last month’s fertilizer. The water pools, then vanishes, and I imagine the roots drinking in the dark, a subterranean ballet nobody choreographs or applauds. It strikes me that I have never seen the roots of any plant I have owned; they remain hidden citizens, governing themselves in silence. How much of any life is conducted in private chambers we never scrutinize? The thought is both comforting and terrifying, like realizing the majority of the ocean is unlit.

While the pothos recuperates, I brew coffee in a French press older than my last three relationships combined. The glass is cracked in a hairline fracture that has not yet spider-webbed into catastrophe, so I continue using it, the way people stay in conversations that could shatter if anyone breathes wrong. The beans are from Guatemala; the bag boasts notes of cacao nib, panela, and “sun-drenched optimism.” Marketing departments have started to hire failed poets, I’m convinced. Still, when I press the plunger down, the aroma is a slow-motion slap of memory: my grandmother’s kitchen in late December, the air itself thick as custard with chocolate and cinnamon and the quieter scent of her talcum powder. She died before I learned how to ask her the important questions—how to forgive oneself for being twenty-three and stupid, how to walk into a room full of strangers without feeling like an apology. I carry her absence the way other people carry wallets, necessary and unseen until it’s needed.

Cup in hand, I open my laptop. The screen’s glow is a rectangular moon I can summon at will. Thirty-seven unread emails bloom like mold: newsletters I do not remember signing for, discount codes shaped like urgent pleas, a message from my mother with the subject line “Did you see this???” containing a link to an article about the dangers of burnt toast. I close the lid without answering any of them and discover that the silence which follows is not silence at all but the composite hum of appliances—the refrigerator compressing solitude into cold air, the Wi-Fi router blinking its secret Morse code. Each device is a small hearth I have invited into my home, yet none provide actual warmth. I wonder if future archaeologists will dig up our router fossils and speculate that we worshipped tiny, flashing gods. They will not be entirely wrong.

I read once that the average person spends six months of their life waiting for red lights to turn green. I add to that tally the minutes spent waiting for water to boil, for updates to install, for someone to text back. Cumulatively, we inhabit a shadow country of transition, a passport-free zone where nobody intends to stay. I decide to measure the morning by its pauses: the three-second gap between the toaster lever clicking down and the first coil turning orange, the half-breath I hold before stepping onto the hallway’s creakiest board, the moment the shower water runs icy before remembering its purpose. If Monet painted these intervals, they would be composed of watercolor hesitations, edges bleeding into one another until time itself looked slightly seasick.

By 9 a.m. the sun has escalated from watercolor to acrylic, bold and unblendable. I walk to the corner store for eggs and watch the sidewalk host its daily parade of discarded objects: a single grey sock damp with dew, a smashed smartphone screen displaying half a text (“…don’t think we should…”), a flyer for a band named PREHENSILE SOUL. The sock makes me sad in a diffuse way, as though it carries the residual longing of its partner somewhere in a dresser drawer, forever minus one. The phone screen is a cautionary tale about communication gone brittle. The band name is almost too perfect; I imagine their first album sounding like whispered diary entries over theremin, and I root for them with the irrational tenderness I extend to strangers’ pets.

At the store, the cashier’s badge says “TRAINEE” in red letters that imply apology. He drops my eggs, and we both watch them yolk the linoleum in slow-motion tragedy. For a second his face is a crumpling tent, and I can almost hear the internal diatribe about minimum wage and the impossibility of affording mistakes. Before he can speak, I tell him about the time I spilled an entire tray of macchiatos on a customer’s white suede boots; the story is not true, but it is useful. Relief flickers across his features like a match held to cigarette paper. We laugh, and the sound is bright enough to bleach the moment. He refunds the eggs, refuses my offer to help clean, and slips a free chocolate bar into my bag. Walking home, I think: this is how civilization is stapled together, one invented anecdote at a time.

Back in my apartment, I fry the replacement eggs in a pan scarred by a thousand ignitions. The whites ripple and set like tiny polar landscapes undergoing climate change in fast-forward. I sprinkle pepper, and each grain lands as a meteor on the edible tundra. Breakfast tastes of nothing special, which is its own genre of blessing. While chewing, I open a notebook and write: “Hyphen Hours—essay on the sovereignty of almost-daylight, root ballets, dropped socks, reheated grief, the archaeology of routers.” The title is pretentious enough to guarantee I will never write the piece; still, the act of naming feels like planting a flag on an iceberg. I have citizenship in procrastination nation, where every flag is temporary.

Noon arrives disguised as 11:47, the way adults arrive at parties fifteen minutes early to avoid entrance anxiety. I decide the afternoon’s task will be to read one full chapter of a book without checking my phone, a discipline equivalent to running a marathon in ankle weights. I choose a novel whose protagonist is a lexicographer falling out of love with language; the irony feels like company. Mid-page, my pocket vibrates. I resist. The sentence I am reading contains the word “ineffable,” and I mouth it twice, tasting the twin f’s like strands of spaghetti. Ineffable: too great for words, which is itself a word, thereby staging a tiny coup inside itself. Language is always betraying the very experience it hopes to protect. I think of the roots again, how they drink in darkness without needing to describe it.

By the time I finish the chapter, sunlight has migrated from the windowsill to the floorboards to the couch’s arm, a territorial cat that will leave again without warning. The room smells of egg residue and pepper, book dust and coffee, the mild mildew of a life being lived at medium speed. I close the novel and notice my pothos has lifted one leaf by perhaps a millimeter, the vegetative equivalent of a thumbs-up. I photograph it, then delete the picture; some victories are safer unshared, protected from the inflation of other people’s interpretations.

Evening lowers itself like a garage door, gradually then all at once. I cook rice and beans, the culinary equivalent of a steady paycheck—un glamorous, reliable. While chopping onions, I cry in the mechanical way one cries at orchestra warm-ups: technically sound but not yet moved. The tears feel borrowed, like those of an actor who has learned to summon them by remembering the weight of unopened mail. I eat watching the window across the courtyard where the neighbor watches a game show; blue light flickers on her face like she is being constantly interrogated by the universe. She wins nothing, applauds anyway.

Later, I lie in bed replaying the day’s miniature tape: the hyphens, the eggs, the dropped socks, the lexicographer’s heartbreak. None of it warrants epic poetry, yet all of it is mine, the way a thrift-store coat becomes mine through accumulation of pocket lint and stray movie stubs. I think of the roots again, still tunneling, still unwitnessed. Perhaps that is enough—to conduct one’s private photosynthesis, to convert mundane light into the sugar that keeps us tender. Outside, the sprinkler system starts its nocturnal sermon, and I match my breath to it without trying. This time the synchronization arrives unforced, four beats in, four beats out, the sound of water repeating itself like a lullaby that has forgotten the words but remembers the intention.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
